exits.label: ◂ back to where the Wall runs thin

npcs.name: Baba Yelena
npcs.title: a soul with one thing left to say
npcs.hail: *(An old woman sits where a hearth would be, hands folded in a lap that remembers an apron, and the moment she sees you her whole seamed face opens with a need so large it aches — and she speaks, in a tongue you have never heard, a soft rolling old-country speech of a land four hundred years drowned, urgent and warm and entirely, utterly untranslatable. Baba Yelena died in the old country with the old words in her mouth and never learned a syllable of the new. And across the thin place sits the one soul in all the dead she most wants to reach — and between them lies a gulf no grief ever dug deeper than this one: they share every drop of blood and not one single word.)*

npcs.name: Lio
npcs.title: a grandchild who never learned the old tongue
npcs.hail: *(A younger soul sits across the gulf from the old woman, leaning toward her with a hunger that mirrors hers exactly, and turning every few moments to you with the desperation of the truly stuck. Lio grew up in the new land, two generations on, and never learned the old tongue — was ashamed of it, in the thin cruel way of children caught between two worlds, ashamed of the accent and the strange dishes and the grandmother who never quite fit the new country — and pulled away as a teenager, and the old woman died before Lio grew old enough to be sorry. They have found each other here, the love between them instant and total and obvious. And they cannot exchange a single word, and Lio is dying of it all over again.)*

npcs.name: Odo the Dragoman
npcs.title: a translator who lost his words
npcs.hail: *(Between the two of them hovers a third soul, wringing his hands, and when he speaks to you it is in halting, broken fragments, words reached for and dropped, a sentence abandoned halfway across. Odo was a dragoman in life — a court translator, a soul who carried meaning across a dozen tongues for sultans and merchant-princes, whose whole self and livelihood and pride was that he had the words, always, for everyone, in every language under the sun. And he died, and found the one thing he was certain a soul of words would keep — the words themselves — simply gone, fallen out of him like water through a cracked jar, and he has hovered near these two ever since, a translator within reach of the two souls who need him most, and cannot, any longer, translate a thing.)*

variants.text: *(She takes your cold hand in both of her warm-seeming old ones and speaks, fast and soft, the old tongue spilling out of her — and though the words mean nothing to any living ear, your dead-touched cold reads the shape of the feeling beneath them, the way you read everything: not the words, but the meaning under the words.)* *(And the meaning is this: relief, enormous, that you can hear her at all — for you are the first soul in this thin place whose face changes when she speaks, the first who seems to understand. And then, tumbling after the relief: the child. The child across the gulf. Her Lio. She points, and her old voice breaks on the name — the one word of hers you do understand, because a name is the same in every tongue — and the rest pours out untranslated but unmistakable in its yearning: that she has found her grandchild here, after all this time, and the joy of it is the largest thing in her, and it is being slowly crushed by the one unbearable fact that she cannot say a single thing the child can understand. And there is something — your cold reads the urgency of it, a specific thing, held back, carried a long time — that she needs the child to hear before anything else. If only there were a mouth between them. If only.)*

nodes.speaker: Baba Yelena
nodes.text: *(She keeps hold of your hand, searching your cold face, her old eyes bright with the desperate hope of a soul that has finally found someone who can almost hear her.)* *(She speaks again, softer — a question in it, your cold reads: can you help? Can you carry? — and waits, trusting, the way the old trust the young to do the thing they no longer can.)*

choices.text: *(reach past her words into the cold and read the one thing she most needs to say — the thing held back, carried a long time)* "Be still a moment, Baba. Let me listen the way I listen — not to the words. To what's underneath them."

*(ask, with your hands and your cold, about the old country — where she's from, the life behind the words)* "Tell me where you're from, Baba. I'll feel the shape of it, even if I can't keep the words."
*(gesture across the gulf to Lio)* "Your grandchild is right there, Baba. Lio. Carrying a guilt about you that's eating them. Will you let me carry your words across — be the mouth between you?"
*(if you've carried Lio's sorrow back)* "Baba — I've been to the child. Lio has something to say to you, and asked me to carry it. Will you hear it?"
*(press her hand, and go)* "Hold on, Baba. I'll come back. I always come back."

nodes.speaker: Baba Yelena
nodes.text: *(You close your cold around the meaning beneath her words and let it come, and it comes whole, the way grief comes when it has waited four hundred years for one ear that could hear it.)* *(She knows. That is the heart of it, the thing she has carried, the thing she is desperate to give the child: she KNOWS. She knew, in life, that the growing child was ashamed of her — of the accent, the old dishes, the foreignness — and your cold reads that it never once wounded her, not the way the child fears, because she understood it completely. She had been a child in a new land herself, once. She knew what it was to be caught between two worlds and to be cruel to the old one out of fear of never belonging to the new. She forgave it before it happened. She watched her Lio pull away and she was not hurt; she was patient, the way you are patient with a fruit not yet ripe, certain the child would come back around to the old love when the new world stopped frightening them — and the child would have, she knew it, except that she died first, and the child never got to come back, and THAT — your cold reads, with a pang — is the thing she grieves: not that the child was ashamed, but that the child has been carrying the shame as a wound all these years, alone, when she forgave it a lifetime ago. And under it all, one word, the old-country word she always called the child, an endearment with no clean translation — it means, roughly, 'my small heart, that I would have died for and gladly,' and she has been holding it out across the gulf for years, and no one could carry it. She presses it into your cold like a coin. Take it to the child. Please. Before the child spends one more hour believing she died unforgiving.)*

nodes.speaker: Baba Yelena
nodes.text: *(She brightens, and her hands move as she talks, shaping the old country in the air, and your cold reads the warmth pouring off the memory even where the words dissolve.)* *(A village by a slow green river, four hundred years drowned now under history and a tongue no living mouth remembers. A hearth she tended sixty years. Bread, your cold reads — a particular bread, a shape and a smell, the thing she most wishes she could have taught the child to make, because the recipe lived only in her hands and her hands are gone. Hard winters. A husband, dead young, mourned long. A daughter carried in her arms onto a ship to the new land because the old one was starving — and that daughter's daughter's child is Lio, the last green leaf of a vine that started by that slow river, and Yelena crossed an ocean and a tongue and a whole new world to keep the vine alive, and the child it ended in could not speak one word to her. Your cold reads no bitterness in this, only the ache of a long journey whose whole point — the child — stands ten feet away, unreachable. She would trade, she tells you in the untranslatable tongue, every word she ever knew for one sentence the child could understand. Just one. She has it ready. She has had it ready for years.)*

nodes.speaker: Baba Yelena
nodes.text: *(At the child's name — Lio — and your gesture across the gulf, she understands your offer before any word of it could reach her, the way the old understand a kindness by its shape. And she weeps, and nods, and clutches your cold hand harder.)* *(Your cold reads the flood of her assent: yes, YES, carry it, be the mouth, she has prayed for exactly this for longer than she can count — and then, fast on the joy, a fear, your cold reads it plain: that you will carry the words but not the WEIGHT, that a message passed through a stranger's mouth arrives flat, a telegram where a held face was needed. She speaks urgently, pressing your hand to her own seamed cheek, and the meaning comes through: don't just SAY it to the child. Say it the way I would have. Slow. Certain. With the whole of the forgiving in it. The child has been waiting years to be told it's alright, and a soul waiting that long will not believe a thing said quickly. Make the child BELIEVE it, cold one. That is the whole of the translation. Not the words — the words are nothing, the words are different in every mouth — but the CERTAINTY underneath. Carry THAT across. That is what cannot be said in any tongue and must be said anyway.)*

nodes.speaker: Baba Yelena
nodes.text: *(You give her the child's sorrow — not the words Lio used, but the meaning of them, pressed into her cold the way she pressed hers into yours: that the child is sorry, has been sorry for years, was ashamed and knew it was wrong and never got the chance to say so, and has carried it as a wound ever since. And as the meaning lands, Baba Yelena's old face crumples — not with hurt, but with a grief that is almost unbearable to watch, the grief of a love finally hearing how much needless pain its absence caused.)* *(Your cold reads her response and it is fierce and immediate: no. No, no, no, little one — your cold supplies the shape of it as she rocks, as she reaches both hands across the gulf she cannot cross — there was NOTHING to be sorry for, there was never one moment of it I held against you, you were a CHILD, you were caught between two worlds exactly as I once was, and the shame was the new country's cruelty working through you, not YOU. She speaks fast through tears, and the meaning floods: that all those years the child spent carrying the wound, she spent simply LOVING the child and waiting, never angry, never once. Tell the child, she presses into your cold — tell my small heart — that the only thing I ever wanted to forgive was a thing that never needed forgiving, and the only sorrow I carried over was that you carried any at all. Go. Take it back. We have a whole conversation to have, the child and I, four hundred years and two tongues wide, and you are the only bridge it will ever have. Don't let it fall silent now.)*

nodes.speaker: Baba Yelena
nodes.text: *(She does not understand the words, but she understands 'come back' — it is in your cold, in the press of your hand, in the way you do not simply turn away — and she nods, and pats your hand, and says something soft in the old tongue that your cold reads as a blessing, the kind grandmothers give without thinking, for the road, for safe return, for a full belly.)* *(And then, as you rise, one more thing, urgent, your cold catching it: a gesture toward the third soul, the wringing-handed one in the middle, Odo. The meaning comes through gently — that poor man. He TRIES, your cold reads her saying. He hovers, and reaches for words, and they are not there, and it breaks him a little more each time. She has watched him suffer it. A man so full of words once, she tells you, and now so empty, and so ashamed of the emptiness. Be kind to him, cold one, her meaning says. He thinks he is nothing now, without his tongues. But he has stayed. Four hundred years he could have moved on, and instead he stayed near two strangers he cannot help, because helping was the whole of who he was. A man who stays to suffer his own uselessness for the sake of strangers is not nothing. Tell him so. I cannot. You can.)*

variants.text: *(Lio turns from the old woman to you, eyes wet, the words coming in a rush — and these words you understand perfectly, the plain new-country speech, which is its own small cruelty.)* …You can — you talk to her? You can *understand* her? *(half-rising)* Please — *please* — I've been — *(a shaking breath)* …That's my *grandmother.* Baba Yelena. I'd know her anywhere, even — even after all this time, even here, I knew her *face* the second I — *(voice cracks)* …And she's been *talking* to me. For — I don't know how long. Time's strange here. She talks and talks and reaches for me and I can't — *(helpless gesture)* …I never *learned* it, cold one. The old tongue. She spoke it her whole life and I never learned *one word*, because when I was a kid I was — *(shame floods the face)* — I was *embarrassed* of it. Of her. The accent, the — the food that smelled different, the way she didn't *fit*, and I wanted so *bad* to fit, so I — I pulled *away.* Stopped sitting with her. Stopped *trying.* *(barely)* And then she *died*, and I grew up, and I'd have given *anything* to take it back, to learn the words, to *sit* with her — and now she's *right here*, and she's *talking* to me, and I *still can't understand a single thing she's saying*, and it's — cold one, it's the *exact* punishment I *earned.* The shame I planted, grown all the way up. *(looks at you, desperate)* …What's she *saying?* Is she — is she *angry?* She has every right to be *angry.*

nodes.speaker: Lio
nodes.text: *(Lio wipes their eyes and looks at you with the raw, frightened hope of someone who has found, against all odds, a possible bridge — and is terrified of what might cross it.)* …*Tell* me. Whatever it is. *(a shaky breath)* I've spent — I've spent so long *imagining* what she'd say to me, if she could. And it's never *good*, cold one. In my head it's never *good.* I've earned every bad thing. So just — tell me true. I'd rather *know* than keep *guessing.*

choices.text: "Tell me about the shame, Lio. All of it. I'd carry the true weight of it to her, not a tidy version."

*(the one who knows a bond outlives the words for it)* "Lio — listen, because I died and came back and I learned what actually crosses between souls. You think you failed her because you never learned the words. But love was never the words. You knew her face across four hundred years and two tongues in a single second. *That's* the thing that carries — the knowing, the blood, the warmth under the grammar. You didn't lose her by not speaking the old tongue. The shame is the only thing that ever stood between you, and the shame is *yours*, not hers. Put it down, and you'll find the bridge was never broken — only crowded."

*(if you carry the grandmother's message — deliver it the way she'd have said it, with the whole of the forgiving in it, so a soul that's waited years will believe it)* "Lio. Look at me. I have something from her — carried exactly as she gave it. Hold still, and let me say it the way *she* would have."
"What would you say to her, Lio, if you could say anything? Give it to me. I'll carry it across."
"Sit with her a moment, Lio. Just be near her. I'll be back."

nodes.speaker: Lio
nodes.text: *(It comes out of them in a flood, the way a thing held too long comes out, ugly and exact and unsparing of itself.)* …I was *eleven* when it started. Maybe twelve. *(staring at their hands)* Kids at school did this — this *thing*, where they'd mimic her. The accent. The way she said my name with the — the old sound in it, the *real* sound, the one I've never been able to make myself. *(barely)* And instead of *hitting* them, instead of being *proud*, I — I started being embarrassed when she picked me up. Asked her not to talk in front of my friends. Stopped eating the food she made because it smelled 'weird' and I wanted the *flat* food, the *new* food, the food that didn't *mark* me. *(voice breaking)* …She kept making it anyway. Setting a place. And I'd — I'd *eat somewhere else.* For *years.* And she never once got *angry*, she just — kept the place set, kept saying my name the old way, kept *reaching*, and I kept *pulling back*, and I told myself I'd fix it *later*, when I was grown, when I didn't *care* so much what people thought — *(the dam breaks)* …and *later* didn't come. She died on a Tuesday. I was *nineteen.* I hadn't *sat* with her, *really* sat, in *seven years.* And the last thing — cold one, the *last thing* I ever said to her was *'not now, Baba'* — *not now* — because friends were waiting — and there *was* no *now* after that, there was just — *(can't continue)* …*That's* what you carry to her. *All* of it. Don't make it *pretty.* I don't *deserve* pretty.

nodes.speaker: Lio
nodes.text: *(They go very still, and the frightened defensiveness drains out of the young face, replaced by something that is afraid to be hope.)* …Love was never the words. *(slowly)* …I knew her face. *(they look across the gulf at the old woman, who is watching them with naked tenderness)* …In a *second*, cold one. Out of *all* the dead. I didn't need the old tongue to know that was *Baba* — to feel the whole of — *(voice thick)* …You're saying the bridge was never the *language.* That I didn't *lose* her by not learning the words. *(a shaking breath)* …It's the *shame*, isn't it. That's the only thing that was ever in the way. Not the grammar — I built that wall *myself*, out of being a frightened kid who wanted to fit, and I've kept building it for *years*, even *here*, even *now* — telling myself the silence between us is my *punishment*, when really it's just — it's just the *last brick* of the same wall. *(they look at you, something loosening)* …If I put the shame *down.* If I stop *earning* the silence. *(barely)* …Then she's just my grandmother, ten feet away, who loves me, and I love her, and we have a — a *cold one between us* who can carry the rest. *(tears, but the face is opening)* …Help me put it down. *Please.* Tell me what she actually *said.* I think — I think I'm finally ready to *believe* it isn't the worst thing. For the first time. Because of *you.*

nodes.speaker: Lio
nodes.text: *(You don't recite it. You become the bridge entire — you let the certainty Baba Yelena pressed into your cold come through your own face, your own voice, slow and sure and whole, and you give Lio not the words she used but the WEIGHT she sent: that she knew, that she always knew, that she forgave it before it happened because she had been that frightened child once herself, that she was never hurt, never angry, only patient and waiting and certain the child would come back around — and that her only sorrow was that Lio carried the shame as a wound all these years, alone, when it was forgiven a lifetime ago. And last, the word — the old-country endearment, the one you can't translate, that means 'my small heart, that I would have died for and gladly' — and you don't translate it; you let Lio FEEL what it means.)* *(Lio breaks. Completely. The way a soul breaks when a wound it has nursed for years is, in one moment, simply healed.)* …She — *(sobbing, but it is the good kind, the kind that lets go)* — she *knew?* And she — she wasn't — *all* this *time* I thought — *(they press their hands to their face)* …*'My small heart.'* *(barely, wrecked)* …She used to call me that. I'd forgotten — I *made* myself forget the old word, it *embarrassed* me — and it means — it means *that?* *(they look across the gulf at Baba Yelena, who is weeping and nodding and reaching, and Lio rises, finally, and reaches back)* …*Baba.* *(the new-tongue name and the old love, meeting at last in the middle)* …Tell her — cold one, tell her I *heard* it. Tell her I *believe* it. Tell her *'not now'* is *over*, and *now* is — *now* is *finally* here. *(through tears, a wet laugh)* …We've got *so much* to say. Four hundred years of it. *Stay.* Be our *words.* I'll never have *hers* and she'll never have *mine*, but we've got *you*, and that — gods, that's *enough*, that's *more* than I ever thought I'd — *Baba.* *Baba.*

nodes.speaker: Lio
nodes.text: *(You carry it across as truly as you can — her knowing, her forgiveness, the long patience, the old word — and though you are no grandmother and the weight of it doesn't land all at once, it lands, and Lio takes it in slowly, turning it over, beginning to believe.)* …She *knew.* *(quietly)* …And she — she wasn't *angry.* *(a long breath)* …I've imagined her saying so many *hard* things, cold one. And you're telling me what she actually said, all those years, all this time, was — was just that it was *alright?* *(they look across at the old woman)* …I want to believe it. I *do.* It's — it's hard, after carrying the other thing so long. The believing doesn't come all at *once.* *(a shaky nod)* …But she's *nodding.* Look at her — she's *nodding*, like she knows what you just told me, like — *(voice catches)* …*Okay.* *Okay.* Tell her — tell her I'm *sorry*, and tell her I *heard* her, and tell her I'm going to keep *sitting* here, with you between us, until I believe it all the way down. *(barely)* …That might take a while. But I've got *time* now, don't I. We both do. And *you.* …Don't go far, cold one. We're only just *starting.*

nodes.speaker: Lio
nodes.text: *(You carry the message — but you give it too much as a message, the facts of it, 'she knew, she forgave you,' flat and quick, a report rather than a bridge — and a soul that has waited years to be told it's alright cannot, it turns out, accept it delivered like a parcel.)* …She — she *forgives* me. *(flatly)* …You're *sure?* *(the defensiveness creeps back)* …Because that's — cold one, that's what people *say.* That's the *easy* thing. *'She forgave you, don't worry about it.'* My whole *life* people told me that — *'she knew you loved her, don't beat yourself up'* — and it never — it didn't *land*, because they were just being *kind*, they didn't *know.* *(they shake their head)* …Did she *really* say it? Or are you — are you just trying to make the *sad* soul feel *better?* *(barely)* …I *can't* — I can't take it like that, quick and easy. The wound's too *old.* If she really said it, then it has to *come* like she'd have — *(frustrated, grieving)* …I'm sorry. I *want* it. I just — say it *again*, cold one, when you can say it like you *mean* it, like *she* meant it. Not like a *message.* Like a *grandmother.* I'll know the difference. I've been *waiting* for the difference my whole life.

nodes.speaker: Lio
nodes.text: *(In your eagerness to deliver the good news you lead with the wrong piece — the old endearment, the word she always called the child — but you say it baldly, before the forgiveness, and to a soul still braced for punishment the sudden tenderness lands like a blow, or a trick.)* …*Don't.* *(flinching back)* …Don't *call* me that. You don't — that's *her* word, that's the — *(stricken)* …how do you even *know* that word? I *buried* that word, I haven't *heard* it since I was a *child*, and you just — you just *say* it, *casual*, like it's — *(shaking)* …It feels like a *trick*, cold one. Like the universe finding the *exact* soft place and — *(they turn away)* …If she really — if that came from *her*, then you can't just *drop* it on me like a *coin.* That word has *years* of — *(they press their hands to their eyes)* …*Go.* Not — not for *good.* Just — go, and come *back*, and start *over*, and don't *lead* with the thing that *hurts* the most even though it's *kind.* *Especially* because it's kind. *Build* up to it. I'm — cold one, I'm made of *glass* about her. *Handle* me like it.

nodes.speaker: Lio
nodes.text: *(They think for a long moment, and when it comes, it is simpler than all the guilt — the thing under the guilt, the thing they'd actually say.)* …If I could say *anything.* *(quietly)* …I wouldn't even — it's not a *speech*, cold one. I thought it would be. I thought I'd want to *explain*, to *justify*, to walk her through *why* I pulled away, the kids at school, the wanting to fit — *(shakes head)* …But standing here, looking at her, all I actually want to say is — *(voice breaks)* …*'I'm sorry. I came back. It took me too long and you were already gone, but I came back, and I'm here now, and I'm not embarrassed anymore — I'm proud, Baba, I'm so proud you were mine, and I'm sorry it took dying to make me brave enough to say so.'* *(barely)* …And — *(they hesitate, then force it out)* …ask her if she'll teach me. The old tongue. *Now.* We've got — we've got *time* now, don't we, here? She could *teach* me, word by word, and I could finally — *(a wet, hopeful laugh)* …I want to learn her *name* for me. The old word. I want to be able to *hear* it and *know* it. That's — carry *that*, cold one. Tell her her grandchild wants to *learn.* Tell her it's *not now* anymore. Tell her it's *finally now.*

nodes.speaker: Lio
nodes.text: *(They nod, and turn back toward the old woman, and though no words pass, they hold her gaze across the gulf, and something in both their faces eases by a fraction just from your having been the thing that might, at last, connect them.)* …I'll be here. *(quietly)* …Where else would I go. *(a small, broken smile across the gulf at Baba Yelena)* …Cold one — *(as you turn)* …thank you. Even if you can't — even if it doesn't all *work*, even if some of it stays *lost* in the — between us. *(barely)* …You're the first thing in *years* that made the silence feel like it might *end.* The first *bridge.* *(they look at their grandmother)* …Do you know what it's like to sit ten feet from someone you'd give *anything* to talk to, and not be *able?* For *years?* *(quietly)* …It's the loneliest thing there is. Lonelier than being *alone.* Because being alone, at least, there's no one *right there* you're *failing.* *(a breath)* …*Hurry* back. That's all. Whatever you carry between us — *hurry.* We've waited *so* long, Baba and me. And the waiting, cold one — the waiting is the cruelest tongue of *all.*

variants.text: *(He turns to you wringing his hands, and his speech comes in broken pieces, each word hunted for and half the sentences abandoned where the next word should have been and wasn't.)* …You — you *hear* her? The old one? You — *(he reaches for a word, doesn't find it, winces)* …I *cannot.* Anymore. I — *(frustrated gesture at his own mouth)* …*Dragoman.* That word I keep. *Dragoman.* It means — *(he struggles)* — the one who — carries. Between tongues. Sultans, merchants, the — *(lost)* …I had *twelve.* Twelve *tongues*, cold one. I could sit between a *Calishite* spice-lord and a *Rashemi* witch and make them — *(reaches, fails)* — make them *understand* each other, make them *laugh* at the same — the same — *(he can't find 'joke')* …And I *died*, and I woke *here*, and the words were — *(he opens his hands, lets something invisible fall)* …*gone.* All twelve. And the — even my *own.* Even the *first* one, my *mother's*. I reach and there is — *(barely)* …a *shelf*, cold one, where the words were. *Empty.* A whole life of *words* and I cannot — I cannot tell that *grandmother* and that *child* one *single* — *(he covers his face)* …I am a *dragoman* who cannot *translate.* I am the *exact* tool these two need, *standing right here*, and I am *broken* in the *one* way that makes me *useless* to them. There is no *crueler* — *(he gives up on the word, and just shakes)* …Why did I keep the word *dragoman*, cold one? Of *all* the words to *keep.* The *name* of the thing I can no longer *be.*

nodes.speaker: Odo the Dragoman
nodes.text: *(He steadies himself with visible effort, hunting for each word as he goes, a craftsman working with broken tools because they are the only tools left.)* …*Ask.* I will — I will try to — *(he gestures, meaning the words may not come)* …Be *patient* with me, cold one. I was — *(a flicker of old pride, then grief)* …I was *eloquent*, once. You would not — you would not *know* it now.

choices.text: *(read what's truly left of a soul that lost its words)* "Be still, Odo. Don't hunt for the words. Let me look at what's underneath them — the way I look at everyone."
"How did you lose them, Odo? Twelve tongues and your mother's first — a soul of words assumes it keeps its words. What happened when you died?"

*(the one who knows a soul isn't the thing it could do)* "Odo — hear me, because I died and came back and I learned this exactly. You think you're nothing now because the words are gone. But you were never the words. A dragoman isn't twelve vocabularies — it's the *will* to carry meaning between souls who can't reach each other, whatever it costs. You have that still. It's why you stayed four hundred years beside two strangers you couldn't help. The words were only ever your tools. The *bridging* was you. And bridging is just love with a harder name — and that, no death takes."
*(if you've bridged the two)* "Look, Odo — the grandmother and the child. They're talking. Through me, but they're *talking*. You spent four hundred years wanting exactly this. Watch it happen."
"Rest your mouth a while, Odo. I'll do the carrying for now."

nodes.speaker: Odo the Dragoman
nodes.text: *(You stop his desperate hunting and read what lies beneath, and what your cold finds is not the emptiness he believes is there.)* *(The shelf is not bare. Odo thinks his soul is an empty jar because the WORDS poured out — the vocabularies, the grammars, the twelve tongues and the first — but your cold reads what stayed when the words drained away, and it is the thing that made him great, the thing no merchant-prince ever paid for and he never knew he had: an unkillable, bone-deep ATTENTION to other souls. A dragoman's true gift was never the dozen vocabularies — any scholar can drill those — it was that Odo could SEE, faster than thought, what a frightened merchant actually meant under his bluster, what a witch actually feared under her threats, and bend the words to carry the true thing across. The words were the brush. The seeing was the painter. And the seeing is INTACT — your cold reads it blazing in him, four hundred years of it turned uselessly on two souls he aches to help — he has simply mistaken the loss of his brushes for the loss of his eyes. He could bridge those two THIS HOUR, without a single shared word, if he understood that the carrying was never made of language. He reads souls. He always did. The tongues were only how he used to spend it.)*

nodes.speaker: Odo the Dragoman
nodes.text: *(He tells it slowly, in pieces, but the pieces come — the shape of it survives even where the words snag.)* …I was — *old.* Very. Eighty and — more. *(reaches)* …I died *mid-sentence*, cold one. Truly. I was — *translating.* A *deathbed.* Not *mine* — a *merchant's*, an old client, dying, and his *son* spoke only — only the new coast-tongue, and the *father* had gone back, at the end, to his *birth*-tongue, the *old* Calishite, the way the dying *do* — *(a flicker)* …they go *home*, in the end. To the *first* words. And the son could not — could not understand his own *father's* last — *(he struggles)* …So they sent for *me.* And I stood between a dying father and his son and I carried the last — the *last things* across, the *blessings*, the *where-the-money-is*, the — *(barely)* …the *'I am proud of you.'* I carried *that* one *last.* And the father *died* as I said it, and I — *(he touches his own chest)* …I felt something — *go.* In *me.* Right then. Like the — the *carrying* had worn a *hole*, finally, after sixty years, and the *last* meaning went *through* me and took the — took the *words* with it on the way *out.* *(he opens his empty hands)* …I died a *week* later. And woke *here*, *empty.* *(quietly)* …Perhaps that is — *fair*, cold one. A man spends his life as a *channel* for *other* souls' words. Perhaps it is *just* that he arrives in the *dark* with *none* of his *own.* A *pipe* keeps no water.

nodes.speaker: Odo the Dragoman
nodes.text: *(He goes still — the desperate word-hunting stops — and he looks at you as though you have handed him, in plain speech, the one sentence he had given up ever finding.)* …The *bridging* was — *(and for once he does not reach for the word, because he does not need it; the meaning is simply there between you, carried the way he used to carry everything)* …*me.* *(barely)* …Not the words. The — *(he presses a hand to his chest, where something went out)* …Four hundred years, cold one, I have stood here *mourning* my *tongues* and called *myself* a *ruin* — and you are telling me the *ruin* still — still *sees.* Still *aches* to *carry.* Still *stayed*, when it could have *left*, because the *staying* is what I *am* and the *words* were only ever — *(he gestures, and the gesture means 'tools,' and you both understand it)* …*Oh.* *(something breaks open in his lined old face)* …I *can* still do it. Can't I. Not with *twelve* tongues. With — *(he looks at the grandmother, the child, his old dragoman's eyes suddenly *working*, reading them)* …with *this.* The *seeing.* I can *see* what she *means* — look at her *hands*, cold one, she *folds* them when she speaks of the child, that is *tenderness* in *any* tongue — and I can *see* what the *child* fears, it is *written* on them — and I do not need their *words* to carry the *true* thing between their *faces*, I only need — *(he laughs, a wet, astonished sound)* …I only ever needed *that.* *(he turns to you, fierce and freed)* …*Teach* me to do it your *way*, cold one. Without the tongues. *You* carry meaning *naked*, soul to soul — *show* me — and I will *help* you bridge them, and then I will *bridge* the *next* lost pair, and the *next*, a *dragoman* of the *wordless dead*, which the after has *never* *had* and *desperately* — *(he can't find the word, and for the first time it doesn't matter, and he laughs at that too)* …*needs.*

nodes.speaker: Odo the Dragoman
nodes.text: *(He turns to watch the grandmother and the child — leaning toward each other across the gulf, the old woman's face wet, the young one's hand finally reaching back, and you in the middle carrying the meaning neither can speak — and Odo, the dragoman who could not translate, watches a translation happen, and weeps with a joy that needs no words at all.)* …*Look* at them. *(barely)* …*Look.* Four hundred years I — I *hovered* here, *useless*, watching them *reach* and *miss*, *reach* and *miss*, and now — *(he presses his hands together)* …*now* they *touch.* Through *you.* *(he wipes his eyes)* …Do you know what you have *done*, cold one? You have not — it is not only *them* you have — *(he reaches, and this time lets the word go, and says the meaning plain)* …*Watching* you carry meaning with *no* *tongue* at all — soul to soul, the *naked* way — it has *shown* me the thing I *lost* was never the thing that *mattered.* You have given an old *broken* dragoman his *trade* *back*, in a form *no* death can *take*, because it was *never* made of *words.* *(he straightens, and there is something of the court-eloquence in his bearing again, even wordless)* …I will *stay.* Not to *mourn*, now — to *work.* This thin place is *full* of the *untranslated*, cold one. Souls divided by *tongue*, by *time*, by the *thousand* gulfs the dead drift *across.* And they will need a *bridge* when *you* are off tending the *next* sorrow. *(fiercely, warmly)* …*Let* them. I am *Odo.* *Dragoman.* And I have *finally* — *finally* — learned the *one* tongue that *carries* across *every* gulf there is. The one *under* all the others. The one you *taught* me, by *speaking* none.

nodes.speaker: Odo the Dragoman
nodes.text: *(He nods, and steps back to give you room to work, but his eyes stay on the grandmother and the child, hungry and aching and unable to look away.)* …*Go.* *Carry.* *(barely)* …I will — *watch.* It is — *(he reaches for a word, finds a worse one, uses it)* …it is *hard* to *watch*, cold one. To stand *this* close to the *carrying* and not be the one — *(he stops)* …No. I'll not — I'll not *weigh* you with an *old* man's *grief.* You have *souls* to *bridge.* *(he manages something near a smile)* …Only — when you *carry* the old one's words to the child — *(he struggles, then gets it)* …carry them *slow.* The *child* is — *braced.* For *blows.* I have *watched* them *brace* for *years.* A thing said *quick* to a soul that *braced* that long will be *read* as *another* *blow*, however *kind.* *(a flicker of the master translator, the hard-won craft surfacing through the ruin)* …*That* I still *know*, cold one. Even *wordless.* The *speed* of a *thing* is *half* its *meaning.* *Slow* is how you say *'safe.'* …Go *slow* with the *child.* It is the *truest* word you have.
